author: @growleym504
text:
3D printers are great for prototyping stuff, because there is nothing quite like holding a thing in your hands to verify the design parameters and suitability for the intended function. I do this even when I will be making the finished part on the mill or lathe. It is also nice for making a mold plug, for investment casting. Just make everything a few thou oversize to allow for polishing. And sometimes plastic is "good enough" for a part or device. 

It just so happens that I received a Neptune 4 Max the day before yesterday. Like you I was pleased with the packing, and the partially complete assembly. The size is very satisfying. I had one other 500x500 machine but the design was ill conceived, and it liked to pinch its own wires and burn out the control board. I always said I would use the bones for a CAM router/engraver but I ended up melting down the aluminum instead. So I have been wanting another big printer. 

The main issue I have is that the included out of date Cura version already set up for this printer is for WinDOHs and Mac only. I prefer a grownup operating system, and I do not use Wine. As often as Wine works, it doesn't work at all. I do not have any WinDOHs computers. MS pissed on my parade too many times and I was with them off and on since DOS 3.  Cura can be downloaded for Linux of course, but configuration files from the Elegoo are sadly lacking for this printer, and you can manually configure for days and not get it quite right. I am sure I will get it, I just have to match together a proper slicer app and a proper config file. Printing the provided g-code models that came on the thumb drive went off without a hitch, so the problem is not in the hardware. I just have to be able to feed it good g-code that doesn't make the printer choke up and throw errors, and that makes a proper top and bottom. 

Elegoof really does need to pay attention to the fact that most hard core DIYers and tinkerers prefer Linux, and even MS techs usually use Linux on their workstations. Pretending that Linux and Unix users don't exit or are unimportant is gonna bite them in the hiney.

Setting the print speed too high makes the printer dance around the workbench like a dry boiler with a fresh dose of feedwater. My workshop is so full of stuff I have to step outside just to change my mind. So I set up the machine in the wife's sewing room. Fair enough, since she doesn't actually sew, anyway. I set it up on a fairly heavy duty folding table and it jitters and shudders and jumps and skips all over the place at over 100 mm/sec. So a substantial workbench is needed, to really push this printer. 

Good unbox review, but I was hoping that you would have printed something with it.

Okay back to work. Still trying to make Neptune 4 Max flavored g-code.

author: @rhotix403
text: I dont know if you leveled properly but those tolerances arent good. You dont want to have 0.3mm and more being compensated as your print will always turn out crooked. Thats just fine for decoration prints or something like that, but not for functional prints. Its not that hard to get milled plates (beds) in that size with a tolerance at 0.1 mm. You just need to mill it down from both sides on an magnetic holder. As chinese prices are cheap it wouldnt be a problem to adress 30 bucks more for a totally flat bed. We need to make them understand that instead of getting crooked ones like this...

author: @DavidSmith-dm8ew
text: I just bought a new neptune 4 and I can't print off anything but the files that came on the usb stick, I can't get any STL files to open, and if I remove the gcode file from the buda folder it doesn't see that STL file either. This is my first printer and I'm lost... why is this difficult?

replies.author: @wk5199
replies.text: You need a "slicing" software on your computer to convert the STL files for the printer to use.  Something like Cura, Orcaslicer,  Prusaslicer, or Elegoo's version. Select printer type or build volume if it doesn't have a  preloaded one. Load the STL file into the program, select the filament type you using, change whatever parameters you want, then hit slice to see how long it'll take. Send to printer when you ready.

replies.author: @DavidSmith-dm8ew
replies.text: @wk5199 Awesome thank you.

author: @Liberty4Ever
text:
I just got my new Neptune 4 Pro yesterday and I still need to get it on my WiFi network.  I'm envious of your Neptune 4 Max that shipped with WiFi.  Kudos to Elegoo for listening to customers and quickly addressing that one deficiency.

I didn't use the Auxiliary bed leveling to pre-level over each adjustment screw.  I ran the 121 point auto bed level, looked at the calibration map and adjusted the four adjustment screws accordingly.  I ran the automatic bed level again and made slight adjustments.  The third automatic bed leveling had all offsets 0.13 mm or less.  The center two columns (where I print the most) had one 0.06 mm and 0.04 mm offset and the other 20 points were all 0.03 or less.  The fact that I was able to quickly level the bed so accurately speaks well to the design and build quality.  There is very little bed level compensation for Klipper to do.

author: @Liberty4Ever
text:
I got my Neptune 4 Pro yesterday.  I assembled it and printed the nicest Benchy I've ever seen... in 22 minutes.  It's amazing how far 3D printing technology has advanced in the last two years.  I need to set up PrusaSlicer for the Neptune 4, and then I'll print some PETG and short ABS parts with a draft shield if necessary, but mostly, I'll be printing TPU parts for my online business.  The high speed doesn't apply to TPU but I expect much better print quality from the more precise Klipper controlled motion and much better part cooling.

The Neptune 4 Plus and Max are releasing tomorrow (they have WiFi!).  I'd love to have a Max (420 X 420 mm) version of the Neptune 4 Pro, but I'm waiting for the Pro version with the steel rails.

replies.author: @plameterms7972
replies.text: Did you get any good results with PETG and ABS without an enclosure?

replies.author: @Liberty4Ever
replies.text:
@plameterms7972 - It was my first time printing with PETG and it printed fine without an enclosure.  The prints look shiny which doesn't look as good in my opinion as the matte finish I get with PLA or ABS, but PETG printed easily and the parts are strong.  They look a bit like injection molded nylon.

I was able to print flat ABS parts with a draft shield when my office was warm but when it was cold outside a draft rolling down the closed window caused the ABS parts to detach from the 100 C bed when they were 3-4 mm high.  I have been printing non-stop ABS parts for the past week, using the following settings:

My new ABS Secret Sauce:

    110 C   Print Bed
    255 C   Extruder - first layer
    230 C   Extruder - subsequent layers
    Textured PEI print bed
    30 gallon clear polyethylene trash bag draped over the printer

I could probably use the above recipe to print ABS without an enclosure in a temperature controlled draft free area such as a closet or small room, but the trash bag enclosure makes the printing very reliable.  It looks redneck but it's as easy as opening a door on an enclosed printer, without the cost, desk space or maintenance difficulties associated with an enclosed printer.

Inside the trash bag enclosure, the air is 110 F to 120 F, depending on the temperature in my office.  There is no issue with print reliability.  I'll start a three hour print and go to bed without waiting to ensure that the first layer adhered well and the parts are waiting for me in the morning.

After the printing has concluded, the parts start cracking off the bed around 70 C and I can gently pull them from the textured PEI bed at 50 C.  I peel off the nozzle prime stripe and press the button on the Neptune 4 Pro screen that's asking if I'd like to print another.  That makes manufacturing very easy.  I'm 44 parts into a 196 part job (6.6 kg of filament).

author: @evo-labs
text: If a company is going to produce a 'fast' printer, a 'bed slinger' design (I3 style) isn't a good solution. A design with a bed that only moves up and down or doesn't need to move at all e.g. CoreXY will always be better because of the lack of bed inertia. And the bigger the bed, the worse the inertia.

author: @richspillman4191
text: I went to a trade show in 1993 where I saw my first 3D scanner/printer...price tag was $22,000.00. A customer of mine bought one, he scanned and printed a penny blown up to 10 times its original size and never used it again. He owned a lost wax casting shop that did extensive work for Bell Helicopter and other military customers. He was riding on his father's coat tails and drove the business into the ground, so to speak. I bought a super cheap unit 3 years ago for $130.00 and learned that it would have been more economical to spend another $50.00 - $70.00 to avoid the headaches of CHEAP. The controller just gave out and I'm in the market for a new one, I will have a look, the timing is perfect.
